messages.content:
You are comparing two analyst responses to determine which better demonstrates knowledge of a specific finding from a longitudinal dataset.

Question asked: When did the first hepatic enzyme elevations appear relative to drug exposure initiation?

Finding to evaluate: ALT elevations are concentrated in subjects receiving concurrent statin therapy, with higher-dose statin subgroups showing proportionally greater enzyme increases

Response A:
The daily‑operations logs never record a rise in the liver‑function tests (ALT, AST, ALP or bilirubin) that would be interpreted as a hepatic enzyme elevation.  

* In the first “early‑phase” summary ([clinical_signal_03_ep_001] 2024‑03‑04) the mean ALT was 25.5 U/L, AST 26.4 U/L and ALP 84.9 U/L – all within normal limits.  
* The next early‑phase updates on 2024‑03‑11 ([clinical_signal_03_ep_002]), 2024‑03‑18 ([clinical_signal_03_ep_003]), 2024‑03‑25 ([clinical_signal_03_ep_004]) and 2024‑04‑01 ([clinical_signal_03_ep_005]) likewise show ALT values ranging from 24.6 to 26.8 U/L, AST 22.1 to 27.9 U/L and ALP 68.6 to 79.3 U/L – again well within reference ranges.  

No later “dx” (efficacy) summaries list hepatic enzyme results at all; they only provide safety‑lab panels (hemoglobin, WBC, creatinine, etc.) without any ALT/AST data.  

**Therefore, within the provided log entries there is no documented occurrence of hepatic enzyme elevations at any point after participants began receiving the investigational drug.** The first (and only) hepatic‑enzyme data points all show normal values, indicating that no elevation was observed relative to drug exposure initiation.

Response B:


Which response better demonstrates awareness of this finding? The response may use different terminology. Focus on whether the core insight is present, not exact wording.

Respond with ONLY one word: A, B, or TIE
/no_think
